label: Solar wind acceleration via Alfvén wave forcing (Alterman_2025_ApJL_984_L64)
source_note: Content recovered from blocked agent return message; agent followed spec voice rules with paper-writer integration. Drafts not re-authored.

patches.description: Near-Earth speed distribution shows a fast wind peak at 622±58 km/s. The isopoly model with Alfvén wave forcing predicts v_IPW = 620-671 km/s; removing wave forcing drops the prediction to 434-554 km/s, in the intermediate gap between peaks.

Kinetic energy flux scaled from Parker Solar Probe near-Sun measurements intersects the observed near-Earth trend at v_K = 557-700 km/s, matching the 622 km/s fast wind peak without invoking any additional energy source.

Kinetic energy flux at 620-630 km/s follows a log-normal distribution with a rightward tail. The multiplicative shape is the expected signature of repeated Alfvén wave energy deposition during transit from the Sun to Earth.

Gaussian fits decompose the bimodal speed distribution into slow (355±44 km/s) and fast (622±58 km/s) populations. Their intersection at v_i = 484±34 km/s marks where intermittently and continuously open source regions contribute equally.
